title: JPMC Center for Geopolitics: Beyond the Benchmarks — U.S.-China AI Competition
executive_summary: JPMorganChase Center for Geopolitics (May 2026) reframes U.S.-China AI rivalry from a model-benchmark race into a systemic contest across seven dimensions — Policy, Hardware, Models & Software, Energy (technological) plus Finance, Socioeconomics, Military & Security (systemic). Core verdict: the U.S. keeps a meaningful overall lead, but it is narrower, more contested and more fragile than consensus assumes — and China is gaining precisely in the dimensions hardest to reverse (energy, diffusion, supply-chain chokepoints).

Two different theories of victory: the U.S. bets on frontier capability (Stargate, energy permitting reform, July 2025 AI Action Plan, $490bn private AI capex in 2025), while China bets on diffusion at scale — 'good enough' low-cost open-source models, the 2017 New Generation AI Plan and 15th Five-Year Plan targeting 'AI+' across the economy, and Digital Silk Road exports across Southeast Asia, Africa and Latin America. Anthropic's Mythos (cyber-vulnerability discovery beyond human experts) is dubbed a 'reverse DeepSeek' moment escalating stakes.

Hardware is the clearest U.S. advantage: 53.7 GW installed data-center capacity vs China's 31.9 GW (India 4th-largest at 3.6 GW), plus allied control (Taiwan, Japan, Netherlands, South Korea) of fabrication and EUV lithography, which China cannot access. Huawei's Ascend 910C/950-series and CloudMatrix rack-scale clusters approach top performance on paper but face manufacturing bottlenecks; JPM Research estimates Chinese lithography (SMEE, AMEC) will not threaten ASML for at least a decade; up to 80% of China's data centers may sit idle.

Model gap is narrower than benchmarks suggest: Chinese and U.S. model downloads reached global parity in 2025; Chinese firms (Z.ai, Alibaba, DeepSeek, Moonshot, Meituan) hold the top five open-weight ranks on LMArena as of Oct 2025; DeepSeek V4 trails the frontier only 3-6 months and is the first model optimized for Huawei's Ascend chip, loosening Nvidia dependence. Distillation threatens to compress U.S. frontier advantage into temporary leads.

Energy is China's expanding edge and the report's most striking data: China generated 10,707 TWh of electricity in 2025 vs 4,670 TWh in the U.S.; since 2019 China added 2,500+ TWh of generation vs just 221 TWh in the U.S.; Chinese average power cost was $0.08/kWh in Q4 2025 vs U.S. $0.18/kWh; BloombergNEF sees China adding 3.4 TW of capacity by 2030 — nearly 6x the U.S. — while 7 in 10 Americans oppose local AI data centers (Gallup).

Finance: overwhelming U.S. capital advantage — 75% of global AI venture capital in 2025 vs China's 5% (OECD); U.S. firms' $490bn AI capex vs China's $98bn — but capex outpaces revenue in both countries and macro productivity gains have not yet materialized. J.P. Morgan Research puts tech-sector bond issuance at 4% of total in 2026, rising to a 'manageable' 10% by 2030; the more the boom is debt-financed (SaaS credit stress, private-credit software exposure already flagged), the more exposed the financial system if returns disappoint.

Socioeconomic and military dimensions: McKinsey estimates current AI could automate 57% of U.S. work hours; Anthropic's CEO warns of up to 50% of entry-level white-collar job losses; China's youth unemployment stands at 16.9% vs 8.5% in the U.S., with Chinese courts ruling firms cannot lay off workers on AI grounds. In security, 73% of cyber leaders already see significant AI-threat impact (Darktrace); norms lag capabilities — neither the U.S. nor China signed the Feb 2026 REAIM declaration, though a Trump-Xi May 2026 Beijing summit established a bilateral AI dialogue.

Scenarios for business (Figure I): base case is 'progress and fragmentation' — a policy-fragmented tech landscape where AI returns justify continued investment; upside is innovation-oriented policy competition with broad-based gains; downside is 'security transcends economics' — financing pullback, uneven hardware access and displacement without reform. Other countries (including India) 'retain agency': their technology-stack alignment choices will shape both AI outcomes and broader political-economic ties.

key_takeaways.takeaway: AI capex has become a macroeconomic and credit variable, not a tech-sector story: $490bn U.S. AI spend in 2025 with capex outpacing revenue means the durability of hyperscaler cash flows now underpins a growing slice of equity valuations and bond supply.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: If AI returns disappoint, the correction transmits through corporate bond markets, private credit and equity wealth effects simultaneously. Track J.P. Morgan's flagged metric: tech-sector share of total bond issuance — 4% in 2026, projected 10% by 2030; a faster climb signals debt-financed froth.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Debt-financing of the AI buildout is the emerging systemic-risk channel — SaaS credit deterioration and private credit's larger-than-advertised exposure to ailing software borrowers are early cracks.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Software/SaaS lending was underwritten on recurring-revenue stability that AI itself now disrupts. For credit work, monitor hyperscaler capex guidance revisions, data-center contracting delays and private-credit marks on software books — the report names these as the first signals the investment thesis is softening.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Energy, not chips, is becoming the binding constraint on AI-driven growth: China's $0.08/kWh power vs U.S. $0.18/kWh and its 6x capacity-addition pace shift long-run competitiveness toward energy-abundant geographies.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Structurally bullish for utilities, grid equipment, transformers and power developers globally (including India's 13-15%-type demand growth analog); U.S. data-center power demand growing 13-15%/yr through 2030 supports long-tenor infrastructure credit. Track national average industrial power tariffs and generation-capacity additions as the leading indicator of AI competitiveness.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Cheap 'good enough' Chinese open-source models winning diffusion (download parity with U.S. models in 2025) compresses AI pricing power — frontier-model monetization, the assumption behind U.S. capex, may not hold.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: If a 90%-as-capable model at a fraction of the cost wins deployments, revenue per token collapses and the $490bn capex base struggles to earn its cost of capital. Track inference-cost trajectories of U.S. vs Chinese models — the report names this the key diffusion indicator.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Export-control and critical-minerals escalation is now a two-way weapon: China's 2025 critical-mineral export restrictions demonstrated chokepoint leverage over the entire AI supply chain, exposing hardware makers and their lenders to sudden input shocks.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Semiconductor, electronics and EV supply chains carry embedded geopolitical event risk that ratings rarely capture. Watch U.S. Congressional action on chip-equipment controls and on-chip location tracking, and any renewed Chinese mineral restrictions; both are named policy watch-items with direct margin and capex implications for issuers.

key_takeaways.takeaway: AI-enabled cyber threat is now a corporate credit factor: Mythos-class models find vulnerabilities beyond human experts, and 73% of cyber leaders already report significant AI-threat impact — banks, energy and infrastructure issuers sit on the front line of interstate rivalry.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Operational-risk and cyber-insurance costs will rise across financials and critical infrastructure; a successful AI-enabled attack on a major bank or grid is a plausible credit event. Track cyber-loss disclosures and regulator responses — the report notes global regulators already reviewing Mythos-related risks with banks.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Labor displacement is a medium-term sovereign-fiscal and consumer-credit issue: 57% of U.S. work hours theoretically automatable and industry leaders forecasting up to 50% entry-level white-collar job losses imply pressure on tax bases, safety nets and household credit quality.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: That AI developers themselves (OpenAI's 'industrial policy for the intelligence age') propose wealth redistribution signals expected disruption scale. For consumer lenders and sovereigns, track employment data in AI-exposed white-collar segments and youth unemployment (China already 16.9% vs U.S. 8.5%).

key_takeaways.takeaway: For non-U.S., non-China economies including India, AI-stack alignment is a strategic credit consideration: which country's models, cloud and hardware a market adopts will shape its access to growth, data-governance regime and geopolitical ties.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: India holds just 3.6 GW of installed data-center capacity vs U.S. 53.7 GW and China 31.9 GW — a large buildout runway that supports Indian data-center, power and telecom credit if energy and capital constraints are managed. Track India data-center capacity additions and which AI stacks (U.S. hyperscaler vs Chinese Digital Silk Road) win regional deployments.

key_takeaways.takeaway: The base case for businesses is 'progress and fragmentation' — continued AI investment within a policy-fragmented global tech landscape; the downside case ('security transcends economics') pairs financing pullback with unmitigated displacement.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Scenario framing matters for portfolio stress testing: in the downside, AI-linked issuers face simultaneous revenue disappointment and restricted market access. Use the report's per-dimension watchlists (export controls, EUV progress, inference costs, capex guidance, employment data) as an early-warning dashboard for regime shift.

entities_impacted.impact: Dominates AI accelerator design and ecosystem stack, but DeepSeek V4 being optimized for Huawei's Ascend chip signals China loosening dependence — a long-term demand and pricing-power risk in the Chinese and Digital Silk Road markets.

Ascend 910C/950PR/950DT chips and CloudMatrix rack-scale clusters approach frontier performance but face EUV-driven manufacturing bottlenecks; success would re-rate China's entire AI hardware supply chain.

EUV monopoly is the single critical chokepoint denying China advanced-node scale; JPM Research sees no Chinese competitive threat (SMEE, AMEC) for at least a decade, protecting its franchise and cash flows.
V4 model trails frontier by only 3-6 months, tops open-weight rankings and undercuts on cost — compresses global AI pricing and challenges the monetization case behind U.S. capex.

Mythos model's cyber-vulnerability capability escalates the security stakes; simultaneously banned from Pentagon contracts and designated a supply-chain risk — illustrating dual-use governance contradictions and regulatory/business-model risk for frontier labs.

entities_impacted.entity: U.S. hyperscalers (AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud, Meta, OpenAI)
entities_impacted.impact: $490bn 2025 AI capex outpacing revenue makes their health a macro variable; capex guidance cuts or data-center contract delays would be the first signal of an AI investment repricing hitting equity and credit markets.
Credit risks building in SaaS borrowers, with private credit's exposure to ailing software companies 'bigger than advertised' — the first identifiable credit-stress channel of the AI disruption.

Electricity is up to 40% of data-center opex; U.S. power demand growing 13-15%/yr through 2030 and China adding 3.4 TW by 2030 create a multi-year secular demand tailwind for generation, grid and transformer credits.
Taiwan leads advanced fabrication, substrates and packaging — the supply chain's geographic concentration near China is flagged as a structural fragility in the U.S. hardware advantage.

Fourth-largest installed data-center base at 3.6 GW; as a non-aligned major market, India's choice of AI stacks and its power-capacity buildout will shape data-center, telecom and utility credit demand and its geoeconomic positioning.

Export controls, investment screening (both inbound and outbound), critical-mineral restrictions and forced restructurings (TikTok, Manus/Meta unwind) are actively redrawing which issuers can access which markets, hardware and capital.

learning: Track leading indicators, not headline capability: grid capacity additions, national power prices, inference costs and bond-issuance mix predict AI competitive shifts years before model benchmarks do — a transferable discipline for rating fast-moving tech-linked issuers.

A debt-financing ratio can be an early systemic-risk gauge for any investment boom: JPM's use of tech share of total bond issuance (4% in 2026 to 10% by 2030) as the 'manageability' threshold is a template for monitoring capex booms in any sector, including Indian infrastructure and data centers.

Cheapest-adequate beats best-available in diffusion economics: a 90%-capable product at a fraction of the cost wins deployments and data flywheels — a caution against rating business models on technology leadership rather than unit-cost competitiveness.

Dual-use technology creates regulatory whiplash risk even for national champions (Anthropic simultaneously indispensable and Pentagon-banned; Manus founders hit with exit bans after a U.S. acquisition) — geopolitical designation risk should be an explicit factor in tech issuer credit assessment.
